speaker: interviewer
text: Leon Borovick is being interviewed. When you were on this march, what-- what-- how did it come to an end?

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, sudden-- they-- didn't any SS men there. The SS men-- the people-- they ran away, the SS men. They left us alone. That's why we knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we were-- it was near Landsberg, I remember. I don't know where we were. We were in houses located near-- there were many empty houses. We were there until the judenrat, that organization made the civil camps. We were in Feldafing there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Feldafing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: About until 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1949. And what-- did you see any Germans on your march? Any civilians? Any--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the Germans were--

speaker: interviewer
text: They evacuated their homes? Is that what you're saying? How did all these homes become vacant?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. There was a war going on, you see. Many people-- I know I found myself with my brother in a certain house. We were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was the first soldier that you saw? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, black people. Black soldiers. They gave us chocolate, I remember. They treated us very nicely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the first time you had seen black people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was shocked, yeah. For a long time when I came to America, it took years until I-- to me, I wonder how people don't wonder looking at them. The black are so different. I had never seen a black person in Europe. So to me, it was-- but I had a good experience in Feldafing. They gave us food and they were very nice to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there in Feldafing?

speaker: interviewee
text: We [INAUDIBLE]-- we used to work for them, I think. I don't remember what we did there. In Feldafing [INAUDIBLE]-- teaching-- I told you, I had courses in Hebrew, Yiddish. [INAUDIBLE] with a theater group. [INAUDIBLE] [? socially. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your plans while you were in this [INAUDIBLE] camp. What were your plans for your future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where, in civil camps?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to go to Israel very much. I didn't want to go to America. My brother-- my brother said to me I-- I cannot go to Israel [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] it's economically very, very difficult there. They had a little baby, 2 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: The baby--where was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was born in camp, yeah. [INAUDIBLE]. My niece.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he met his wife in the camp in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Birkenau, yeah. So he said, you want to go to Israel? Go. [INAUDIBLE] I go to America. So I went with him. They came to my stepbrother in Philadelphia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it took four years before you got your visas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. We waited. During that time, many people went to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the spirit like in the civil camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was interesting. We used to have-- we used to have cultural activities, I said. I even wrote poems. I published with a-- it was a newspaper with Latin letters because we didn't have Jewish-- Yiddish print-- Jewish print-- Hebrew print. So I think I told you that one man found an archive in New York, and one of my poems-- a few poems of mine. And he invited me onto the radio, [INAUDIBLE]. He liked these poems. And I was there on the radio three times, invited there. And I read it. Because I started to write poetry.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your spirit like at the end of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very-- I felt abandoned. I lost my whole world. My whole universe fell apart. And I-- people were-- everything was alien to me. It was-- in the camp, we were clinging to each other, the survivors in the camp. But I went even to [GERMAN] to shows. [INAUDIBLE] some shows. I went there. I liked [INAUDIBLE], Very clean city, a very nice city. These Germans, they have beautiful cities. Beautiful, [GERMAN]. Very beautiful. And-- but yeah, I wrote quite a few poems. When I came to America, I was in the seminary. I was taking a course-- trying to be a teacher. So I published a song in the class journal.

speaker: interviewer
text: What seminary were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Seminary. One year. The last [INAUDIBLE] grade. I graduated and [? that's how-- ?] I used to work in the shops in America the first year or two.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the shops?

speaker: interviewee
text: The shops. All kind. Very hard work. I was a-- I was a presser, I was a pocketbook maker, framer, all kind of things. And then one man gave me good advice. His name was [? Chaim Bass ?]. He was my teacher in the seminary. Before he was my teacher, I came to the work [INAUDIBLE]. He was staying around there, and these [INAUDIBLE] stayed at [? a ?] house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I came to him, and he dealt with the Yiddish culture. I said in Yiddish to him, what should I do? I'm [INAUDIBLE]. I look for work and I can't get any work. I was a teacher in Poland. I didn't tell the man that I was not an official teacher. He said, you know what? And he gave me the advice. He also, in a way, saved my life. He gave me a chance to survive in New York. That city, I didn't have anybody. He said, you go to the seminary because you don't know anyone. Let people know you. Give them a chance to know you. And then you'll get a job. You'll be a teacher.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that time, there were many Jewish schools. My god, if I came now, [INAUDIBLE] Yiddish schools now. There were many Jewish schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say Jewish schools, what grade level?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish, Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yiddish schools.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was a renaissance about 50 years ago. There were many [? labor-Zionist ?] schools, and [INAUDIBLE] and leftist Yiddish Jewish communist schools. Shalom Melachem schools. There were many schools, with teacher's seminary.

speaker: interviewer
text: For high school-aged children or older?

speaker: interviewee
text: All kinds. For little children--

speaker: interviewer
text: And older?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, no. Yes. I was working-- I was in the middle school. I was on three levels-- elementary school and high school, Yiddish, and in the teacher's seminary I was teaching. You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you teach in the teacher's seminary?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was teaching in the Hebrew teacher's seminary at [INAUDIBLE] and in the Yiddish. [INAUDIBLE] first three. They-- They called it a institution of higher learning. It's a kind of a college. [? [HEBREW] ?] they called it. I was teaching [HEBREW] in Hebrew-- Hebrew language. In Jewish teacher's seminary, I was teaching Yiddish and Yiddish literature.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was the only teacher who teaching in a college level without having even a BA. I was the only one. They didn't ask me for-- what do you call it? A certificate or a diploma.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they just took me there. And I liked it. I loved working in the seminary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you taught for-- for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite a few years.

speaker: interviewer
text: In New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: New York. [INAUDIBLE] It was falling apart. It was not that-- now they're together with Touro College, but no, there is no more. There were no more schools. But I was teaching [INAUDIBLE] schools, [INAUDIBLE] schools. In the Kinneret Day School-- that's where my friends are. And they took care of me in case. When I go to the hospital, they always come. They help me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What school is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kinneret. That is where I worked the last 25 years or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother came to Philadelphia, and he became a businessman-- a very successful businessman. Very successful. He bought a house. His daughter married and she has a daughter already. He passed away about 2 and 1/2 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wrote poetry all these many years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Since I came to America, I didn't stop writing. The first poem-- my debut was actually in Philadelphia. As I said, I sent, from Europe, five poems. When I came to America, one of my teachers was a Mr. [? Minkov. ?] He was a teacher in new school for social research. He was a great Yiddish poet. He was my teacher, and he was the editor of [INAUDIBLE], that magazine [INAUDIBLE]. And he published my first poems. And then I was on the radio. People know me. And I wrote in other publications. I wrote a few poems in a Hebrew magazine. And in my school-- a few of my Hebrew poems are part of the curriculum in my school, Kinneret. During the Passover, they recite my poems, Hanukkah, for the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you spoken at all about your Holocaust experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Interesting. I was invited once by a non-Jewish man. Somehow, I said to myself, if he will call me again, I'll go there. I'll accept that. But if he won't call me, then I won't. That's how it was. I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was in the hospital when I had a heart attack, in Einstein. Saw a man-- I saw a man [INAUDIBLE]-- he stood with a wife. And he said to me, how do you feel? And I said, I went through a heart attack, and I ended up in surgery, and I don't feel good. At that time I didn't undergo surgery, but I just had a heart attack. So he spoke to me, and said to me-- so my number. [INAUDIBLE] jacket. We talked about Holocaust. And I told him a few things. He said to me, you know what? I would invite you to my school-- I'm a teacher-- to talk to the children. Did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said fine. But then he gave me a telephone number, and they called me again when I was sick. But then they didn't call me, and I didn't go. But I did in my school. I was invited in Kinneret, and I did talk to the class, the seventh grade, of my experience in Auschwitz. Yeah, all about Auschwitz, I talked.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it that got you to speak now in such-- to speak now about your experiences, so many years later?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean here?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing is that I was very reluctant. And I was even about to call you up to cancel this, really. But I went to the hospital a few days ago. I felt pain here. All of a sudden, pain. It was a strange pain. It was not constant pain, it was every couple of minutes. I used to have, like, a pluck. You know, I was frightened to death. And so I said, maybe it will go away. It didn't go away for two days. I went to Einstein. So before I went there, I was about to call that-- but I said to myself-- I say, don't be in a hurry, you see. Don't be in a hurry. Wait. Be calm. Maybe you shouldn't. The only reason is, it's a duty to me, because it appeals to my sense of responsibility. That's the only reason. Otherwise, I wouldn't go over this. If you gave me a million to do it once more, I wouldn't do. If anyone would call me now, and say-- anyone. If my closest friend told me to go through this procedure, I wouldn't go through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's too much. It's too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: To recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could talk without this thing, without this camera. I was interviewed. I talked to [INAUDIBLE] lady, but that was not about the Holocaust. But I felt more at ease when it was not-- I didn't touch upon the problems. Another thing-- I have to remember these things. Sometimes I don't remember, and I'm responsible. So the only thing I [INAUDIBLE] it because I say I don't know. I don't remember it 100%, but that's the way I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to add to your story? Any message that you would like to give?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the one thing that is a good message-- how to be a Jew. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In Kielbasin, in that camp, Kielbasin, there was a Jewish doctor with a non-Jewish wife, and I think one daughter or two daughters over there. I don't know how they came, how his wife came. She was not a Jew, and she was a Catholic, a Polish. That doctor was a convert. He was Jewish, but he was a convert. So the rumor was in that Kielbasin camp that his wife blamed the man, her husband. Because of him, she has to share his fate, you see, to be together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said that he committed suicide. Maybe he wanted to make it easier for them to survive. Maybe if he won't be alive, maybe they'll free his wife. Know what I mean? That's how the rumor was.

speaker: interviewee
text: It makes no sense to me now. Because if you were there, you couldn't be freed. Nothing could make you free. But the thing-- the fact is he committed suicide. He was lying on the floor and he was dying. While he was dying, people heard three words in Polish. And these three worlds are so-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] meaningful, really. It couldn't be more meaningful, that.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, in Polish, [POLISH]-- a Jew remains a Jew. I can't forget that. I said that at a meeting of teachers, of Hebrew teachers. That is a message, isn't that? So before he died, he said to us, [? my ?] people, a Jew-- it means after all I went through, I became a doctor and I made a career, [INAUDIBLE]. And converted, but still, a Jew is a Jew. What does it mean to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, it reaffirmed my look on Judaism and being a Jew. In America, to me-- and I'm convinced of that-- there's a misconception about being a Jew in America. And I said it to my friend, a lady that interviewed me for Columbia University, the project, that in America-- in America, to be a Jew means you have to-- you have to be a Jew-- you have to hold onto Jewish-- to Jewish religion. You have to be--

speaker: interviewer
text: If you don't-- if you don't obey the [? [HEBREW] ?], that means you're not practicing Judaism. You are not-- you're not a practicing Jew. To me, it is-- still, I can't understand that. How come? It's not-- it's not-- it's not true. That's not a [INAUDIBLE]. That's not the essence of being a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jew means, like, you are a son of your father, of your mother. You are a daughter. So you don't have to practice your [? son-ship, ?] so to speak. You're a son. You are born to the Jewish people, not to the Jewish religion. I am not religious at all. I am not religious. I don't believe in your dogmas. I wrote, even, a poem about that, my credo. I reject all the dogmas. These are blind dogmas. To me, religion, is-- in a way, it showed the invalidity of many religious teachings in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: People praying to God-- where was God? In my poems, of course, I talked to God, and some people think that I'm religious. But that's metaphors. There are many poems to God. [INAUDIBLE] and all kind.

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned a lot in [INAUDIBLE] and I still study Talmud. I like that. To me, this is just curiosity. But to be a Jew, you have to practice Judaism. That means that you are a Jew. Let's say if you're an atheist or agnostic-- [INAUDIBLE] one lady was on TV. She said, I used to be a Jew, but now I'm an Atheist. To me, it is silly. I think [INAUDIBLE] be an atheist [INAUDIBLE], but you can-- you're still a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Religion is-- see, thoughts should be free. Men should be free. You cannot-- just because you are born to a Jewish person who was religious, does that mean you have to be religious? Not at all. You could be. You could not be. I was and I'm not now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Religion has many ethical aspects. I accept that. But there are many things I don't accept. I reject it completely. I say, it's baloney. It's not working in life. [? Didn't ?] [? so-- ?] there was no mercy in Auschwitz. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But might there be something about the Jewish heritage that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Are we still on? Oh my god.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there something--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I said something, maybe, that I shouldn't have said.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you're welcome to say anything. Is there something about the Jewish heritage that you learned as a small child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. I was imbued with that, with all these Jewish values.

speaker: interviewer
text: That makes you who you are. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But it doesn't mean that I have to-- see, I have to-- religion is something that you're educated in. That's religion. And you still, you could believe in that, believe in many things. I believe in the ethical value in some Jewish religion. Fine with me. But many things I reject.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I ask questions. I doubt. See, my mind is free. Does that mean I am not practicing [INAUDIBLE]? That's baloney.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am a  Jew. Like that man said, a Jew is a Jew. See, that's in your blood. That is your everything. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: It is biologically. That is your race. That is your people. To me, the same as I [INAUDIBLE]-- I am a son of my mother [? [GERMAN] ?] and my father, Josef. I can't-- they were Jews and I am a Jew. And I will be a Jew till-- and I'm proud of that. I am more proud than some other people who don't have, let's say, the Yiddish culture. I have, in addition, with the whole Yiddish culture with Yiddish drama, Yiddish-- Yiddish philosophy, Yiddish thinkers, poetry, Yiddish playwrights, folklore-- beautiful folklore. Yiddish, to me, the richest language in the world. You know what I mean? And also, Hebrew. And I love Hebrew narrative and Hebrew poems. Would you like to play something for us that you have composed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. [TUNING MANDOLIN] OK. [PLAYING MANDOLIN]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I go for a few minutes? I think, just the handkerchief there. I'll come back. Can I? Let's stop now. This is a continuation of tape number five.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [TUNING MANDOLIN] [PLAYING MANDOLIN]

speaker: interviewer
text: All right?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PLAYING MANDOLIN]

speaker: interviewer
text: Does that have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is interesting. This-- it is a melody to a poem for my brothers-- for my step-brothers. It's called, in Aramaic, [NON-ENGLISH]. With-- with awe. I can't pronounce it-- A-W-E. With awe and compassion, or something, and love to them. To all my brothers, forgive me for not going to the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in the fire. What do you call that in English, that--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the crematoria?

speaker: interviewee
text: When liquidation took place, they put the Jews to [INAUDIBLE] to be burned. What do you call that?

speaker: interviewer
text: On the stake.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Forgive me for not going with you on the stake. Forgive me for saving my skin. That's in my poem, [? 'My ?] [? Brother'. ?] So I wrote this. This is the high tune so I can sing it. [PLAYING MANDOLIN] [NON-ENGLISH SINGING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where are my glasses? 'Batia' [SPEAKING IN HEBREW] [? 'Lacking ?] reality, I hear, in the silence of the night, the voice of [? Batia, ?] the daughter of [? Shmoyel ?] and [INAUDIBLE]. Escape, I beg of you. I am lost already. And maybe [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] God will spare me. The merciful one didn't-- didn't-- have mercy on her. The faithful shepherd didn't save her life. Our ashes, the wind blow away. My heart is crying over her departure. With reverence-- with sacred reverence-- I [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] dedicate--' yeah, dedicate-- 'you this poem, my love. Your pure memory [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] will-- your pure memory will shine forever, Batia, my spouse, my love. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Verily, to the day of my death, I am with you-- with you, you innocent one. Rest in peace, my--' [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the word [HEBREW]-- my comfort, my consolation, my dear.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That make sense in English?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, it does. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Feldafing, after the war, in a civil camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a picture of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's a picture of me, taken in Feldafing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had regained your strength and your weight.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: You regained your weight and your strength.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's right. This picture was taken in Feldafing, too. To the right-- I am to to right, and the middle one is [? [GERMAN] ?]. He lives now in Los Angeles. He is the manager of a magazine called [? [HEBREW]. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: To the left is-- his name is [? [GERMAN] ?]. We were both active culturally in Feldafing.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my younger brother. He passed away 2 years ago-- 2 and 1/2 years ago, I think. He's in Israel now near the-- near the Wailing Wall, the Kotel Maaravi.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: We called him [? [HEBREW] ?]. By the way, he saved my life in Auschwitz. This picture was taken many years ago. I don't remember exactly when. I think I was teaching then. I don't know where. I don't remember when, but I was a teacher, then, I think, in one of the schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for participating.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. [? [GERMAN] ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good. I feel good now.
